title: Light-Driven Intrinsic Perfect Superconducting Diode Effect
abstract: We demonstrate the perfect superconducting diode effect (SDE) -- unidirectional supercurrent with 100% diode efficiency -- in light-driven nonequilibrium systems. Although the perfect SDE is difficult to achieve in equilibrium, monochromatic light induces the perfect SDE in systems lacking inversion and time-reversal symmetries. More strikingly, multi-frequency light enables the perfect SDE even in centrosymmetric systems via dynamical symmetry breaking. Our results establish a general principle for realizing unidirectional superconducting transport based on nonequilibrium control and symmetry engineering.
summary: This paper shows that light irradiation can be used to engineer unidirectional superconducting transport. By using specific light frequencies, researchers can break symmetries to create a 'perfect' diode effect where current flows in only one direction, even in materials that are normally symmetric.
main_problem: The paper investigates how to achieve the 'perfect' superconducting diode effect (SDE)—where supercurrent flows in only one direction with 100% efficiency—using light-driven nonequilibrium control.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that monochromatic light can induce perfect SDE in systems lacking inversion and time-reversal symmetries, and multi-frequency light can induce it even in centrosymmetric systems via dynamical symmetry breaking.
method: The study employs the Time-Dependent Ginzburg-Landau (TDGL) theory, using perturbative expansions of the order parameter and Fourier analysis of the supercurrent in the presence of AC electric fields.
model_or_system: The model consists of one-dimensional or higher-dimensional superconducting wires subject to continuous-wave light or microwave irradiation, described by a TDGL framework with broken inversion and time-reversal symmetries.
key_observables: Diode efficiency (eta), DC supercurrent (j_dc), photo-induced current (j_photo), and nonreciprocal/nonlinear superfluid density.
important_parameters: Light intensity (E0), frequency (omega), symmetry-breaking coefficients (alpha_3, beta_1), and temperature near the critical temperature (Tc).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the system is near Tc, uses a single-q ansatz where Cooper pair momentum is time-independent, and neglects the imaginary part of the relaxation constant.
figures_summary: Figure 1(a) shows reciprocal DC supercurrent with zero efficiency; supplementary figures show diode efficiency as a function of symmetry-breaking parameters, field amplitude, and frequency.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of perfect SDE, presents the TDGL theoretical framework, analyzes the monochromatic light case in noncentrosymmetric systems, extends the analysis to multi-frequency light in centrosymmetric systems, and discusses the validity and limitations of the approximations used.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The dc supercurrent jdc(q) (blue line) under light irradia- tion. The equilibrium value jeq′(q) (black line) is modulated by the photo-induced current jphoto(q) (red line). The diode efficiency is (a) η = 0 for α3 = 0 and (b) η ∼−0.094 for α3 = 0.2. We set β1 = 0, E0 = 0.01, and ω = 0.1.

FIG. 2. The dc supercurrent jdc(q) and its components jeq′(q) and jphoto(q) are shown by the same color as Fig. 1. The ac electric field is increased from (a) to (d), as E0 = 0.033, 0.052, 0.065, and 0.074. The estimated diode efficiency is η ∼0.152, −0.124, −0.069, and 1.000, respectively. The parameters are the same as in Fig. 1(b) except for E0.
FIG. 3. (a) Diode efficiency η as a function of α3 and E0 with β1 = 0. (b) β1 and E0 dependence of η with α3 = 0. We set ω = 0.1 in both (a) and (b).
FIG. 4. Diode efficiency η in centrosymmetric systems (α3 = 0 and β1 = 0) under two-frequency light. The vector potential is A(t) = A1 cos ωt + An cos nωt with ω = 0.1. (a) n = 2. (b) n = 4.
